docs.title: Adams Python API Reference
docs.text: This is the API reference for the Adams Python API — the import Adams interface embedded in Adams View 2023.1.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import Adams

# Create a model
model = Adams.Models.create(name='MODEL_1')
model.units.length = 'mm'
model.units.mass = 'kg'
model.units.time = 'sec'

# Create a rigid body
part = model.Parts.createRigidBody(name='PART_1')
part.mass = 1.0
part.Markers.create(name='MKR_1', location=[0, 0, 0])

# Run a command
Adams.execute_cmd("simulation single_step dur=1.0 steps=100")

docs.title: How to navigate
docs.text: Use the left sidebar to browse by category, or the search bar to find a specific class, method, or property.

docs.title: Session entry point
docs.text: The Adams module is the top-level session object. All models are accessed through Adams.Models, defaults through Adams.defaults, etc. See Adams and Model for details.

docs.title: Manager pattern
docs.text:
Every collection of child objects is exposed as a typed manager:

model.Parts           # PartManager
model.Parts['PART_1'] # RigidBody
part.Markers          # MarkerManager
part.Markers.create(name='MKR_1', location=[0, 0, 0])  # Marker

See Manager for the base class interface.

docs.title: undo_begin_block
docs.text:
undo_begin_block() -> None

Signals the start of a new undo block in an existing nested set of undo blocks. This allow you to group commands, as you issue them from the command window, into undo blocks. By grouping them into undo blocks, you can use a single Undo command to reverse all the operations in the block. You can define undo blocks around macros, command files, or any group of commands. You can nest them to any level

Example 
Adams.undo_begin_block()

docs.title: undo_end_block
docs.text:
undo_end_block() -> None

Signals the end of the last undo block in the nested set of undo blocks. You can nest undo blocks to any level.

Example 
Adams.undo_end_block()

docs.title: undo
docs.text:
undo() -> None

Allows you to reverse the action of the previous undo-block given to Adams View, so that Adams View appears as it did before the command was originally issued.

Example 
Adams.undo()

docs.title: redo
docs.text:
redo() -> None

Allows you to reverse the action of the last undo backward command, and makes Adams View appear as it did before the reverse action was originally done.

Example 
Adams.redo()

docs.title: switchToCmd
docs.text:
switchToCmd() -> None

Switches the active scripting language to the Adams CMD language in a cmd script having a mix of CMD & Python commands. To switch back to Python, use the CMD language command "language switch_to python".

Example 
Adams.switchToCmd()

docs.title: values_full
docs.text:
values_full(*args) -> ValuesView[Object]

Return all managed objects keyed by their full dot-path name.

Unlike values, this method uses the full dot-path name (e.g. '.MODEL_1.UDE_INSTANCE_1.PART_1') as the dictionary key, so objects from different UDE instances that share the same short name are all returned — none are silently dropped.

Prefer this over values whenever the model contains User-Defined Element (UDE) instances whose child objects share identical short names. See ADMS-137078.

Example: 

# Instead of (may silently drop duplicates under UDEs):
for part in model.Parts.values():
    ...

# Use (returns every part regardless of name collisions):
for part in model.Parts.values_full():
    ...

docs.title: keys_full
docs.text:
keys_full(*args) -> KeysView[str]

Return the full dot-path names of all managed objects.

Unlike keys, this method uses the full dot-path name (e.g. '.MODEL_1.UDE_INSTANCE_1.PART_1') as the key, so objects from different UDE instances that share the same short name are all represented — none are silently dropped.

Prefer this over keys whenever the model contains User-Defined Element (UDE) instances whose child objects share identical short names. See ADMS-137078.

Prefer this over items whenever the model contains User-Defined Element (UDE) instances whose child objects share identical short names. See ADMS-137078.

docs.title: items
docs.text:
items() -> ItemsView[str, Object]

Return (short_name, object) pairs for all managed objects.

Warning: The dictionary is keyed by short name. If the model contains User-Defined Element (UDE) instances whose child objects share the same short name, only one object per name is returned — later entries silently overwrite earlier ones (ADMS-137078). Use items_full to retrieve all objects by full dot-path name.

If the model contains User-Defined Element (UDE) instances whose child objects share the same short name, only one object per name is returned — duplicates are silently dropped (ADMS-137078). Use values_full to retrieve all objects regardless of name collisions.

docs.title: FEPart
docs.text:
FEPart(_DBKey)

Bases: Part

A geometrically non-linear finite element beam part.

.. note:: The CMD nodes_at_curve_points option (which parameterizes node S-values to the reference curve's control points) has no Python API equivalent. FEParts created via Python always behave as if nodes_at_curve_points=no: node locations are fixed at creation and are not automatically updated when the reference curve's control points change.

Provide one of: (i_location and j_location), ref_curve, or coordinates=True (then add nodes via addNodeXYZ).

.. note:: The CMD nodes_at_curve_points option is not available in the Python API. Node S-values are always fixed at creation regardless of the reference curve's control points (equivalent to nodes_at_curve_points=no). To re-mesh, manually call addNode(), modifyNode(), or evenlyDistributeNodes() after modifying the curve.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default name str

Name of the FE part.

None i_location Marker or DesignPoint

Start marker/point of the beam centerline. Required with j_location when not using ref_curve or coordinates.

None j_location Marker or DesignPoint

End marker/point of the beam centerline. Required with i_location when not using ref_curve or coordinates.

None ref_curve Object

Already created B-spline curve to use as the reference centerline.

None coordinates bool

If True, the centerline is defined by x,y,z node coordinates added via addNodeXYZ(). Mutually exclusive with i_location/j_location and ref_curve.

None cratiok float

Fraction of the stiffness matrix contributing to damping (default 1.0).

1.0 cratiom float

Fraction of the mass matrix contributing to damping (default 1.0).

1.0 material_type Object

Material object defining section properties.

None section_label Object

Default Section object applied to all initial nodes.

None fepart_type str

Formulation type. One of '3DBeam', '2DBeamXY', '2DBeamYZ', '2DBeamZX'. Default is '3DBeam'.

None external bool

If True, external geometry is used for rendering. Only external geometry will be rendered.

None vm Marker

Marker along whose axes initial translational velocities are specified.

None vm_name str

Full name of the translational velocity reference marker.

None wm Marker

Marker about which initial angular velocities are specified.

None wm_name str

Full name of the angular velocity reference marker.

None faceting_tolerance float

Faceting tolerance for the FEPart geometry.

None

docs.text:
create(name: str = None, function: str = None, evaluate: str = None, compare: Literal['eq', 'ge', 'le'] = None, value: float = None, error: float = None, angular_value: float = None, angular_error: float = None, time_error: float = None, codgen: bool = None, dt: float = None, halt: bool = None, restart: bool = None, sensor_return: bool = None, yydump: bool = None, sensor_print: bool = None) -> Sensor

summary

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default name str

Name of the sensor

None function str

Specifies a FUNCTION expression to define the sensor.

None evaluate str

Run time function describing the return value

None compare str

Can be "GE", "EQ", or "LE". Specifies what kind of comparison is to be made to initiate the action by the SENSOR.

None value float

Specifies the non-angular VALUE you want to relate to the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing.

None error float

Specifies the absolute non-angular value of allowable error between VALUE and the value of the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing.

None angular_value float

Specifies the angular VALUE you want to relate to the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing.

None angular_error float

Specifies the absolute angular value of allowable error between VALUE and the value of the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing.

None time_error float

Amount of time error allowed for the sensor to trigger

None codgen bool

Specifies that Adams is to generate a new pivot sequence for matrix factorization when the event Adams is sensing has the specified relationship to VALUE.

None dt float

Specifies that the time between consecutive output steps should be redefined. This is done when Adams first senses that the FUNCTION specified has the same relationship as specified to VALUE. Adams uses this value until it is changed.

None halt bool

Specifies that execution should be terminated when the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing, has the specified relationship to VALUE.

None restart bool

Specifies that Adams should restart the integration when the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing has the specified relationship to VALUE. Adams reinitializes the integration step size to HINIT and reduces the integration order to one.

None sensor_return bool

Specifies that Adams should stop the simulation and return to the command level, when the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing has the specified relationship to VALUE.

None yydump bool

Specifies that Adams should dump the state variable vector when the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing has the specified relationship to VALUE.

None sensor_print bool

Specifies that Adams should write data to the request, graphics, and output files when the FUNCTION that Adams is sensing, has the specified relationship to VALUE.

None

Returns:

Type Description Sensor

docs.title: Simulation
docs.text:
Simulation(_DBKey: Any)

Bases: ObjectComment

class for creating and running simple and scripted simulations

Usage 
simulation_object=model_object.Simulations.create(name='sim0', [optional_arguments]) #create a simulation object

Examples:

Run a simple simulation with default end_time and number_of_steps 

mod = Adams.defaults.model

s1 = mod.Simulations.create(name = 'sim1') 
s1.simulate()

Run this simple simulation with specified properties values 

s2 = mod.Simulations.create(name = 'sim2', end_time = 1.25, initial_static = True, number_of_steps = 50)
s2.simulate()

Run simulation using acf commands in sim_test.acf file 

s3 = mod.Simulations.create(name = 'sim3')
s3.script_type = 'solver_commands'
s3.script = "file/command = sim_test.acf"
s3.simulate()

Run simulation with inlined acf commands 

s4_sim_script = ["simulate/transient,duration = 10, dtout = 0.01","linear/statemat,file=model.mat"]
s4 = mod.Simulations.create(name = 'sim4', script_type = 'solver_commands', script = s4_sim_script)
s4.simulate()

Run simulation with inlined cmd 

s5 = mod.Simulations.create(name = 'sim5', script_type = 'commands', script = "simulation single_run transient type = auto_select initial_static = no end_time = 5.0 number_of_steps = 50")
s5.simulate()

objects: List[ObjectBase]

All child objects created by this UDE instance (Parts, Markers, Forces, etc.).

This list can be used as an alternative traversal path when model.Parts misses parts due to short-name collisions between UDE instances (ADMS-137078).

Example::

from Adams import Part
ude_parts = [o for ude in model.UserDefinedInstances.values()
             for o in ude.objects if isinstance(o, Part)]
